title: Carding fleece into rolags
subtitle: Preparing washed fleece for woollen-style spinning with hand cards
excerpt: Hand carding opens and aligns washed fleece fibres into a soft, lofty preparation called a rolag. Rolags are spun from the fold for woollen-style yarn, which is airier and warmer than worsted. This tutorial covers the carding stroke, how to load the cards evenly, and how to roll a rolag.
sourceNotes: Hand card design and the carding stroke are standard in handspinning instruction. Rolag preparation for woollen spinning is well documented in spinning texts. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A soft tube of carded fleece fibres rolled off the hand card. Fibres in a rolag are arranged in a random, airy mass rather than parallel lines. Spinning from the rolag end produces woollen-style yarn.
A natural lock or cluster of fleece fibres as they grow from the sheep. The staple length determines whether a fleece is better suited to worsted or woollen preparation.
A pair of paddle-shaped tools with fine wire teeth used to open and align fleece fibres before spinning. The wire teeth separate the fibres and remove second cuts and vegetable matter.
Yarn spun from a carded, lofty preparation (such as a rolag) in which the fibres are not fully parallel. Woollen-spun yarn traps more air than worsted-spun and is warmer but less durable.

content.text: is a soft tube of opened fleece rolled from a hand card. The fibres in a rolag are lofty and randomly arranged, which produces a warm, airier
yarn when spun from the end.
work best with shorter- to medium-
fleeces (3-8 cm): long staples tangle in the teeth.

attrs.title: Wool dust
attrs.body: Carding fleece produces fine wool dust. Work in a ventilated room. Damp-mop the floor afterwards: never dry-sweep wool dust, which keeps it airborne.

content.text: Hold the left card face up in your non-dominant hand with the handle pointing away from you. Lay small tufts of washed fleece across the teeth, working from the handle end toward the far edge. Load lightly: the teeth should be half-covered, not buried. Overloading produces a poorly carded result and makes the stroke harder. Remove any second cuts (short cut fibres) or obvious vegetable matter before loading.

Hold the right card in your dominant hand, teeth facing down. Place the right card teeth on the loaded left card near the handle and draw it smoothly toward yourself with a light, even stroke. Do not press hard: the teeth should skim the surface of the left card's load without digging into it. Repeat 4-6 times until the fleece transfers largely to the right card and looks open and even.

Reverse the stroke: place the left card teeth-down on the right card and draw away from you to transfer most of the fleece back to the left card. Repeat the forward and reverse stroke 2-3 more times until the fleece is fully opened, fibres are no longer clumped, and the surface looks even and fluffy. Do not over-card: the rolag should be lofty, not flat.

Hold the left card face up. Place the right card face down at the far edge of the fleece on the left card. Roll the right card toward you, peeling the fleece from the left card and rolling it into a loose tube. Slide the rolag off the right card teeth gently. You now have a soft cylinder of prepared fleece ready to spin from the end.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two problems common in hand carding.

items.symptom: Fleece is still clumpy or neppy after carding
items.cause: Either the fleece was not washed and opened before loading, or it was loaded too thickly and the teeth could not reach all fibres.
items.fix: Open locks by hand before loading. Use smaller amounts per card load. Card more passes, finishing with light strokes.

items.cause: Over-carding has separated the fibre too much, or the rolag was rolled too tightly, compressing the air out.
items.fix: Card fewer passes and roll the rolag more loosely. A good rolag is light and can be stretched slightly without breaking.
